full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Welcome back to This Week in Startups. I'm your host, Jason Calacanis. I am just wrapping up an amazing week here in Tokyo where we launched Founder University. Founder University's a pre-accelerator. You would go to Founder University in year zero of your startup. You got two or three co-founders, you built a project, maybe you incorporated, maybe you haven't even bothered incorporating yet. You're in year zero. That's what Founder University's for. It's a 12-week program. We do it in Tokyo, we do it in Saudi Arabia in Riyadh, and we do it in America. We have thousands of people apply and dozens of people we accept. And we spend time with you working on your startup. So while I'm here, I've been recording some episodes of This Week in Startups, TWiST, as we refer to it in town. And I've been learning a lot about the ecosystem in here in Tokyo and as well discussing with a bunch of venture capitalists, angel investors, and founders how they look at the market. Japan, still third or fourth most important economy in the world. And something changed in Japan for the last 20, 30 years. Young people wanted the safety of a job at a big company, and they might work there for 40 years. Now, the preference of young people in Japan has flipped over the last five years or so. They want to start companies. And they've got quite a legacy and a tradition of innovation in the technology space. So I'm spending time here because I think it's going to be one of the great startup markets of the next 20 or 30 years. With me today, I've got two investors and founders. Max Weiss is a venture capitalist, we had dinner last night. His firm is Pacific Bays Capital. He focuses on aerospace, defense, space, energy.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And basically making Japan more resilient, almost Japanese dynamism, to use the term we use in America.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah, I think so. Anything related to economic security, really. And we believe that we need to partner with the US to make those kinds of things happen. So a Japan-focused but also a strong economic security sort of partnership type of focus too.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and this is a very interesting time to be doing that, as I said, or observed from the feedback I got this week. Young people are looking at entrepreneurship in a different way and the relationship with Japan and America is in a little bit of a crossroads. We're going to get into some of that geopolitics as well and the global dynamics. Fred Almeida is also with us. He's an entrepreneur and investor, deep focused on AI and autonomous systems. You're the CEO of American Medical, which is a roll-up. You're...

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's a mix. It's actually two parts of a platform.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Explain, just simply.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so, so we felt actually that there's a shift in the way that medicine is being provided to a lot of people both in US and Japan and it's clear that we're not getting enough doctors coming out of the schools. Especially doctors that have a more general approach to how medicine could be done. Everything's really specialized, especially in the US. And so we felt that the patient side needed their own version of an AI medical doctor. But we also needed to balance that out with the doctors themselves. So we actually started with a number of hospitals and we're already working with doctors to basically figure out how do doctors want to talk to patients through an AI platform.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Got it. And this is under development, launched?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: It's under development. Still pretty new.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, great. So I think that gives us two really interesting jump-off points. And you heard my sort of preamble here about Japan. You're based in Japan?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Tokyo. Right here.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: In Tokyo. Why?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Oh, this is... I mean you've been here many times. It's a lovely country. I actually think, to be honest, like some of my first startups were here in Tokyo and I built one of the first robotics companies here, self-driving car company here in 2016 and that was way before anyone really understood what that was going to mean. And it was just, it just feels right to build AI and technology like robots here. And it just does.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and is part of your thesis about healthcare that AI will have a profound impact on people doing self-directed care?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah, entirely. I actually think that the future of where this is going to go is that the AI will become your doctor in a few more years.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Your primary care doctor?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: It'll be your primary care doctor. And then you'll just go to diagnostic toolsets, which is part of the roll-up. We actually are buying clinics to do diagnostics but at a higher level, if you think about it as almost really changing how a diagnostic clinic will ever work.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Explain in layman's terms what diagnostic clinic means. What are the top five things they do?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Okay, so you'll actually find if you go to a doctor, a lot of doctors don't want to...

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Call for tests because it's really kind of painful for them. They have to fill out forms, they have to deal with a lot of issues with that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Insurance issues?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Insurance, pre-authorization in the US. In Japan, there's like limits to tests.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So it's cumbersome. So they don't send you for tests.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yes, and then so what ends up—you end up spending more time doing like basic testing with the doctor just looking at your ear, for example, if you have a ENT and if you have like, like an infection. And then it may take four or five visits before they actually realize, after they do a diagnostic like a CAT scan or MRI, that you have an infection in your, you know, tonsils instead of your ear, right? And then so the—we believe that if you actually fix diagnostics, there's—it's not perfect diagnostics, right? You do get positive, you know, you get negative positives and positive negatives, right? So ultimately what we want to solve is can we use AI inside the clinics to make it much better? But at a higher level, as a—if you could do a roll up across clinics, you can actually see how people are generally feeling, right? And you can actually extrapolate trends and movements across it. It's in a way like it's the way you would do like a differential geometry of like a stock market, you can see the flow of how people are moving and then you can then make projections on it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So to translate that, I have some pain in my ear or, you know, maybe it's in my throat, I'm not sure. I've got a collection of symptoms. I type into an AI: these are my symptoms. It does a really good job.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it'll actually do differential analysis on you.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and it does an amazing job today. In fact, a lot of the studies that are coming out today say it's matching and exceeding doctors, correct?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yes, we are already are actually, yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: So that's today. Who knows what happens in a couple of years? It'll clearly be better.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Then the second piece is, okay, there's this weird thing where the system is so convoluted that a doctor might not want to send you to just get the test that would confirm this. But if you take out the doctor visit, which is how they make their money, which they're incentivized to say, 'no, come to the office, come to the office,' and that wastes tons of money and time, why not just ask the AI, 'hey, here are the symptoms.' It says, 'you need to go get a swab in your throat or a blood test, whatever it happens to be,' and then we'll know. So that's more efficient. So you're actually flipping it and saying just go right to the test.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: And the benefit of if you can go to the test early enough, we can catch disease at a level where you can treat it with very easy—like an easier way to treating it. So if you—if I find cancer at stage one, it's very fast, right? It's—you can literally not have cancer by just doing a special test. And on top of that, most people don't realize that the reason you're feeling bad is often you just don't have enough nutrients.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Huh.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: And if you do, for example, a genetic methylation test, we can actually see what you're missing.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: And then you just did it. And then so you don't even have to go to the doctors at that point.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That's fascinating.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So this is extremely disruptive. There's a group of people I'm wondering if you've been inspired by, the tip of the spear biohackers, body hackers, self-directed folks who are using their Whoop, their Oura, their 8 Sleep, we're investors in that last one, and then they're going to Function Health or Whoop is doing labs in this, in the United States at least, and then Superpower is another lab one, I did that one and they're proactively getting their blood work, using a concierge or AI to sort of analyze it, and then for me, like, the primary care doctor seems like the worst modality. Why not just get your blood work done fully twice a year and be ahead of the curve?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah, no. And in fact, if you actually think about it, it's actually even cheaper even in the United States to proactively get your diagnostics done. And then because we're trying to change the way diagnostics are done too, so like if you can imagine 500 clinics across the United States all using AI to make you, let's say you're getting a very simple blood test, a doctor calls for like certain markers, but we can do the whole thing for you. And then we can get that, you know, somewhat paid for by the government because they want the data, the metadata, not your data, the metadata. And now we can actually then look at all your markers.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: And maybe we see something, and we can immediately tell the doctor what we see. So he gets a differential diagnostic from us saying hey, we found this. And then right now because of the way the laws work, you have to go see a doctor to get a prescription, but that's going to change.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: So this is all incentives. The incentives are broken, the system is backwards, and we could, there is a better way, and the incentives need to change.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yes, it's backwards.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: And insurance needs to change.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And insurance needs change. And what a more delightful experience for the doctor to say hey, here are your hundred patients as a primary care doctor, here's their dashboard, we have done their blood work twice a year, we've done these other tests, here's their complaints and what happened, now when you meet with them, you're starting with this, you're starting with 80% of the work done, you can actually do better work, you can move up the stack and do higher level, more rewarding work.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: So what we've found when talking to doctors, we've talked to a lot of doctors in the US and in Japan, and a lot of very young talented doctors want to quit.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Why?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: They hate being a doctor because most of their day is doing paperwork or talking on the phone with an insurance rep. To the point where like sometimes doctors are in surgery and they get called out of the surgery to argue with insurance company because they don't want to pay for the surgery.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: It's insane.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: While they're in the surgery.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: That type of stuff drives them mad. And considering we have such a small group of doctors in Japan and in the US, you can't drive them insane. We have to treat them like Formula One drivers from my point of view.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: system that automatically creates all the paperwork that you need, removes the government side of it, removes the insurance side of it. All you have to do is just confirm that diagnosis that you had and then this system that we're creating for patients ensures the patient follows through with what you want them to do. Right? We remind you to take your pills, which pill when, this one take this now. Right? So it's not to the point where like we're not yet creating like this perfect medical doctor that's going to sit in your pocket, but that will come out.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Right. And so if we can create...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Got it.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So Max, let's move over to your business. Why did you pick, you know, Fred picked a really hard one, healthcare, lots of regulations, lots of incumbents, lots of regulations, insurance, just so much friction. You also picked an area with a lot of friction: going to space, atoms and bits. You know, the bits are easy, the atoms are hard. Aerospace and defense, energy. And in a very volatile world, I assume the volatility and the vol creates the opportunity. But tell me why you picked the verticals you're investing in.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: For us it's, it's more of a kind of looking at what Japan has to offer and going backwards from there. So I think this was talked about a little bit earlier at some point. I believe we're in the middle of decade two in venture in Japan. Uh the first decade was defined by consumer and software as a service as I'm sure it was in lots of other parts of the world. But then you had this whole dynamic there. Now we're in a situation where you know maybe maybe according to some people this is decade one depending on how you look at it. So the amount of financing, the amount of support, all this stuff is slowly getting to a global scale. So maybe you can call it decade one depending on how you look at it. But basically I think a lot of stuff changed and now you're looking at folks who used to work at multinational corporations and that's really where a lot of the technical talent lies and where I think Japan's sort of hidden strength lies. And those guys, what they're doing is materials and semiconductors and that's why we focus on the areas that those could be applied to.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, when we get back from this quick break, I want to talk specifically about defense here in Japan. And Japan has an army or doesn't have an army? When we get back on This Week in Startups.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, we're back here in Tokyo, wrapping up my first week of Founder University, did Angel University as well. My guest today, Max Weiss from Pacific Bays Capital, and Fred Almeida, who is with American Medical, doing a roll up and using AI to really reinvent healthcare. Max, you and I had some conversations last night over some outstanding wagyu and—

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: That was outstanding.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Crazy. We went to this amazing wagyu place and I said, 'You can't put wagyu on a barbecue, it'll light on fire. I've cooked wagyu many times.' And he said, 'Oh, this grill is a very hot grill, and the grill then has water running through the tubes where you would lay the meat on top of so that it doesn't light on fire.'

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that was kind of a novel thing that I hadn't seen before either, but it went really well with the meat. You don't overcook it, and yeah, it was great.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, and then you decided you found out I was paying and you did the caviar and the truffle supplement.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Exactly, yeah. Just stuffed myself.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: It was crazy. It was fantastic. I mean, if we had that dinner with the exchange rate and with just importing wagyu to the United States, it would have been three times as much. I got the bill and I thought it was the wrong table's bill. I was like, 'This can't be right. This has got to be double.' And it was not. And we had just such a spectacular meal and a spectacular conversation that eventually got to the relationship between America and Japan. This is a long-standing, you know, and in our lifetimes, absolutely fantastic relationship. But part of that is the United States provides the defensive capability in the military for Japan. But Japan also has fighter jets and military equipment and they know how to use those. So Japan doesn't have a standing army—

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Not officially.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Not officially. But they do. But does have individuals who know how to operate military equipment.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Exactly. Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: See, I have a high tolerance for ambiguity, I understand exactly what's happening here. So, but the relationship between the two countries of late, America seems super focused on our hemisphere, the Western. China has a certain concept of their relationship here in this region. You have Korea, Japan, Singapore, and then Australia, New Zealand forming some type of alliance or group. But how do the Japanese people look at the relationship with America?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: American, it's here, we're on TWiST so you can have permission to be candid. What do you think of our changing stance in the world order? And then how does the average person in Japan think about their protection and their safety when North Korea is shooting rockets over the island?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: I think that's a good question. I'll preface this by saying I don't think a lot of ordinary Japanese citizens are political, like they tend to be back in the US. That said, you can kind of see these general trends. So for example over the past couple of years, I think we're seeing a shift to the right with the new sort of prime minister and you've had these sort of changes to how Japan interprets the self-defense force that it has, which is what it's officially called, although by any practical means you look at it and say this could be a military with the snap of a finger, right?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So the people don't like to talk about politics. It's not the blood sport that it is in the United States, but the leadership here is acutely aware of, hey, the United States and their loyalty to NATO has been questioned, maybe we'll even pull out of it, maybe we don't need to be involved. So there is a sense like, hey, we might need to go it alone.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Potentially. I think that's a little bit less a part of the discourse right now. There's this growing sort of reluctance towards immigration, for example, things like that. So it could be a matter of time before the conversation does turn into things like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So this is something we're talking about, and we're aware of, and you're aware of because of your investment thesis, but maybe not the populace yet.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I would say that there is a change in that I think Japan does think it's going to have to do it alone. And I not entirely...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Japan being the people of Japan or the government?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: No, the government, because they're looking at what's happening in Europe, they're looking at how America is dealing with the stresses of what's happening with Russia and Ukraine, and what's happening in Venezuela, and now Greenland. So they're looking at that and they're wondering, you know, maybe we should invest more heavily into our defense. Now they're not being offensive. I don't think Japan's going to go take over countries or anything like that, but there is a push, you can see one is they're deciding not to import low-skilled workers. What they really want to do now is actually invest heavily into AI robotics and start building probably something more like Lucky Palmer's doing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, my pal. Palmer Luckey. Friend of the pod.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah, he's great. Yeah, so like really what I would love to see in Japan is a lot of new Lucky Palmers, right? And then building...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Palmer Luckey, sorry.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: He's also lucky. Or good.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah. I think that might have been one of the things you were hinting at, and it's not that we're explicitly investing in defense...

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: tech in Japan, I think that's more of of what we look at in the US.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: There are a lot of of sort of technologies, for example, semiconductors and chips, that could be used in satellites and other defense things like that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: And those can be found here. But it's I think they're less likely to call it defense tech than they are everywhere else.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Since we kind of dovetailed into immigration, another and it's I don't bring these topics up because I want to talk about politics here on this We Startups. It's because startups are driving politics and it's hard to tell sometimes which is downstream of the other. Is politics downstream of the technological innovation or is politics driving technological innovation? I it's kind of like these things are in orbit and there's gravitational pulls. Japan has traditionally been a very closed society and you can come here, but you're not going to become a citizen. But with population decline and the need to take care of older people and to fill jobs, immigration's one solution. But what we're scared of in America, job displacement, robo-taxis and Waymos taking all these jobs, China also very nervous about losing all those driving jobs, that is an opportunity here. It's looked at differently.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: That's right. So many people who are driving taxis who are 70 years old.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Actually just today, he was 80.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Who's 80? Driving a taxi.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I love those guys. Strap those in. No, I mean, these guys know exactly where they're going.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Automation's never really been a dirty word here, like it's been in other countries. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Fascinating that it's looked at as an actual opportunity and I think Waymo's driving some cars around and Uber has made a couple of announcements.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Waymo's here and a few others, I think Zoox might be here.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Zoox. Sorry, Zoox.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Zoox? Sorry, Zoox.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Yeah, Zoox.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: When you're looking at those companies, tell me about Japanese startup culture and the Japanese capacity to build these type of companies because there's a big tradition...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: ...here of doing it? Yeah. And do you have enough factory workers to do it or is it going to be kind of, as I was talking on the program about Uniqlo, you know, maybe people were making clothes here on the island previously, but now hey, we're designing it, we're sourcing the fabrics, we're building the brand, we're building the technology for checkout all that other stuff, but we'll we'll build it in another country. So what's the Japanese capabilities right now when it comes to defense tech, deep tech, etc.?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: You brought up Uniqlo which is interesting because I think this is somewhat of a well-known story, but they were laughed out of the room because they described themselves as a technology company even though obviously they make clothes. But look where they are now. And I think that's what you'll see in a lot of cases here. The technology exists and it's great and top of the line. So up until now you could basically, you know, use Claude code in different kind of solutions like inside of your IDE or whatnot, but now they're basically saying you have to stay inside of the ecosystem. You cannot use it with cursor, you cannot use it with Google's development tool they just released, I can't remember what it's called. Anti-gravity, that's it. The trend now seems to be that most of the close-source providers are going to move to ecosystem protections, right? So if you're basing... I mean, I guess the real question is like, how would you get this to work if, for example, your customer is based on Accenture or...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay, great.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: I would say primarily the main value is that it's behaving and testing the application as if it were a user. So the system can't actually tell whether it's a user or if it's our AI, right? So platform is separate. The other kind of strategic importance I feel is separation of concerns. As you know, quality is like—in this case, quality assurance system like this is kind of like a doctor in a way, right? And so...

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Needs to have separation from the building of the thing. So if you put all the context inside the build system, it creates some really odd results where it tells itself it works correctly, but there's no actual external monitor to validate that. So that's my main angle, Cloud QA that provides that monitor at scale separate from the core systems that you used to develop them.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Great. Max, you have a question for one of the two?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: So I feel like with media social companies, you know, a rising tide floats all boats, and I saw at the end they have 4 million downloads, so I apologize if you've already covered this, I might have been missing something. But I'd be curious to kind of understand the problem-solution in the context of where is all the sort of movement happening right now in the space that you operate, and you know, where's sort of the magic that you provide that allows you to grow to where you are now?

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Basically we are mostly organic. That's how big the need is, seriously. So I personally do SEO and ASO myself, App Store SEO sort of. So if you are in Japan, you type Japanese, I'm confident you can find me if you want to chat with someone in Japan, not in the US. I need Jason's new name to do that in the English. Yeah. Actually, not just me, many there are many service there. Because the need is growing and nobody care of them because all people here has so many friends. How many friends do you have?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Too many.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Too many, like you get at least 20 name card today, I guess. So, but that's the truth and that's why democracy doesn't work now, you know. Maybe it's too big, but it's because a lot of people they have no friend. People say you are average of five friends, right? But they don't have five friends. So people with agenda get into their kind of brain, basically. So that's how some of them, they they try to find a way to find their friend and the fastest way is to search, talk to someone online, and that's where I catch the need.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: My question is more along the lines of, so for example, you mentioned a few social platforms, but one way to find friends is on Bumble, for example. It's not necessarily just for romance. And then you also have Clubhouse. It's a good way to meet new people, right? So I'm curious about just sort of with these options, clearly there's some sort of trend in which a lot of Japanese people are gravitating towards some form of media or meeting people and where you sit.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They're almost hacking existing systems to solve the problem. Do you think? Like, because Clubhouse isn't meant for making new friends. It's meant for conversations around topics. But that's interesting that you say in Japan people are using it to make friends.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: Well, as much as people still use it, I think.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: That's a real interesting case study.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: So regarding Clubhouse and also I think Naval, a famous—

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: WeChat. Yeah. And it's kind of similar, I think. And also Bumble. All of them require, kind of encourage you to bring your real identity to the platform.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Use your name, use your photo. And again, the 30% just—just they don't want their real identity in. They want a new start, I suppose. Or they just want something quick.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I think this is the magic of what you've done. You have realized there is a cold start problem, and you've realized let's just take all the friction out. But Chatroulette wasn't explicit, it was more for entertainment, not for friendship. And then I like your idea of the big reveal. We invested in a company many years ago called Whisper that was a geography-based one, so you would start chatting with people who were in your neighborhood. And these anonymous chats do have some issues. You have to be careful about, you know, maybe going after young kids, etcetera. So you're going to have a lot of issues you're going to have to deal with. But if you're thoughtful about it, I think that you have the start of something that could be magical, which is, okay, you start with no persona, then you do a persona.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Then you can reveal your real self when you want, and both people can choose to reveal themselves. And that could only be for paid accounts.

full_transcript.text: Yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So if you want to reveal, you have to be a subscriber and then what you can say is you will verify their identity.

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Identity.

You've verified their phone number, you've verified their email, you've verified their driver's license, all different ways, credit card. You'd at least know something about the person to avoid some problems. I think it could be magical. It's weird, it's interesting, and I think you're the right person to do it.

full_transcript.text: Let's give all five companies a big round of applause! And now I ask you to pick your one and two. Your one and two. We'll start with your two and then go to your one. We'll go two, two, two, one, one, one.

full_transcript.text: whole software development life cycle, but I still have tons of questions but I do like it. And my number two—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Okay. Your number two.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Max, who's your number two?

full_transcript.speaker: Max Weiss
full_transcript.text: My number two would—I think it's the same company, the AI-powered Q&A.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So AirTesting was your number two.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I considered that for my number two, I considered AltSource Global as my number two, and I considered Cascade as my number two, but I picked for my number two Matt and GoMochi. I think it has the lowest chance of succeeding of the five, but I think if it does succeed, it could have a profound impact. In other words, the execution is going to be extremely hard for you, Matt. full_transcript.text: Now, Max, your number one.

full_transcript.text: Sure. So, uh, just caviatting this, agreeing with you—

full_transcript.text: I did not.

full_transcript.text: Okay.

full_transcript.text: Yeah, AltSource Global.

full_transcript.text: Yes, AltSource Global. Um, and I just put a lot of—of emphasis and—and weight on does this company have to be from Japan, then from Japan does this have a chance of becoming a global company? I kind of emphasized on that.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Oh, I like your framework.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Yeah, great job, Max.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Fred, who's your number one?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis

full_transcript.speaker: Fred Almeida
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.text: All right. There you have it, another amazing episode of Twist is in the can. See you next time. Bye-bye.

highlights: AI is projected to become the primary care doctor of the future, enabling a shift toward self-directed care where patients go directly to diagnostic clinics based on AI analysis rather than waiting for traditional doctor referrals.

Japan’s technology landscape is shifting toward "Japanese dynamism" in aerospace, defense, and semiconductors, utilizing its legacy of technical talent to build companies that address national and economic security.

While the West often fears job displacement from AI, Japan views automation and robotics as essential solutions for its labor shortages and aging population, seeing technology as an opportunity rather than a threat.

A significant barrier to innovation in Japan is "Gentenshugi," a cultural philosophy where individuals are judged by their mistakes rather than their accomplishments, fostering a risk-aversion that entrepreneurs must actively work to break.

Successful startups are often driven by a "founder’s secret," a unique and non-obvious insight into a problem—such as the massive demand for anonymous connection or the critical need for transparent supply chain risk analysis.
summary:
This transcript from *This Week in Startups* features host Jason Calacanis in Tokyo, exploring the evolving Japanese startup ecosystem alongside investors Max Weiss and Fred Almeida. The conversation centers on a significant cultural shift in Japan, where younger generations are increasingly choosing entrepreneurship over traditional lifetime employment at major corporations. A major theme is "Japanese dynamism," with the group discussing how Japan’s unique challenges—such as an aging population, labor shortages, and a history of risk aversion—are actually creating a fertile ground for AI, robotics, and aerospace innovation. Unlike in the West, automation is viewed here as a necessary solution rather than a threat to jobs.

The discussion also delves into the disruption of healthcare and global supply chains. Fred Almeida outlines a thesis where AI becomes the primary care provider, streamlining diagnostics to bypass the administrative friction and "burnout" currently plaguing doctors in both the U.S. and Japan. Geopolitically, the guests discuss Japan’s need for greater economic security and technological self-reliance. They highlight the "English allergy" and the traditional *Gentenshugi* philosophy—where individuals are judged by their mistakes—as hurdles that new, cross-cultural "hybrid" teams are beginning to overcome by blending Japanese technical discipline with American-style audacity.

The episode concludes with a pitch session featuring five "year zero" startups. These companies presented AI-driven solutions for ad optimization (Cascades), supply chain risk (AltSource Global), life coaching (GoMochi), social isolation (Random Chat), and software testing (Airtest). The investors favored AltSource Global for its alignment with Japan’s manufacturing strengths and global scalability, while also noting the high-upside potential of "weird" consumer apps like Random Chat. Ultimately, the transcript highlights Tokyo as a burgeoning tech hub poised to leverage its deep technical legacy to build the next generation of global companies.
